text: Reproduce it as the real actor first: `sudo -u auditor /usr/local/bin/read-audit-log`. Compare that with running it as root/trainee -- does it work differently depending on who runs it?

`ls -l /usr/local/bin/read-audit-log` shows `-rwxr-xr-x root root` -- executable, root-owned, looks fine at a glance. Look more carefully at the permission string itself, specifically the owner's execute position.

A helper meant to let a non-root user touch a root-only file needs the setuid bit (an 's' in the owner-execute slot, e.g. `-rwsr-xr-x`) so it runs with root's effective privileges regardless of who invokes it. It's currently missing.
Restore it: `sudo chmod u+s /usr/local/bin/read-audit-log`. Confirm with `ls -l` (owner-execute shows `s`), then re-run it as `auditor`.
